_meta.authoringNotes: Numbers and recommendations cross-checked against AAOS Clinical Practice Guideline on Meniscus Tears (2024), ESCAPE trial (van de Graaf 2018), METEOR trial (Katz 2013), Sihvonen FIDELITY trial NEJM 2013 PMID 24369076, ISAKOS meniscus consensus (Kopf 2020), and Cochrane meniscal repair review (Beaufils 2017).

h1Title: Medial Meniscus Tear: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: A medial meniscus tear is the most common intra-articular knee injury, occurring when the C-shaped fibrocartilage cushion on the inner side of the knee splits under shear, compression, or twisting load. The medial meniscus is torn roughly three times more often than the lateral meniscus because it is firmly anchored to the medial collateral ligament and capsule, restricting its movement during pivot. Tears fall into two clinical groups: acute traumatic tears in athletes (often paired with anterior cruciate ligament rupture in the so-called unhappy triad) and degenerative tears in adults over 40 that develop without identifiable injury. Patients present with medial joint-line pain, swelling within 24 hours, clicking, catching, or mechanical locking when a displaced fragment blocks the joint. MRI confirms the diagnosis with about 95% accuracy. Treatment ranges from rehabilitation alone (first-line for degenerative tears) to arthroscopic repair or partial meniscectomy.

keyStats.prevalence: Meniscal abnormalities on knee MRI: 19% of women and 56% of men aged 50-90 (Englund 2008 NEJM); incident symptomatic tears 60-70 per 100,000 person-years
keyStats.demographics: Acute tears: male athletes 20-30; degenerative tears: adults over 40, equal sex distribution; medial-to-lateral ratio approximately 3:1
keyStats.avgAge: Acute tears typically age 20-35; degenerative tears typically age 40-65
keyStats.globalCases: Meniscectomy is the most common orthopedic procedure worldwide, performed roughly 2 million times annually; partial meniscectomy still accounts for over 700,000 US procedures per year despite METEOR trial findings

definition: Medial Meniscus Derangement (ICD-10: M23.2), with S83.21 covering traumatic tear variants, is a structural disruption of the medial meniscus, the crescent-shaped fibrocartilaginous wedge that sits between the medial femoral condyle and the medial tibial plateau. The meniscus distributes axial load across the joint surface, deepens the tibial socket, stabilizes the knee against rotation, and lubricates articular cartilage. Tears disrupt these functions: contact stress on the underlying cartilage rises by 235% after total meniscectomy, accelerating osteoarthritis. The medial meniscus is anatomically tethered to the deep medial collateral ligament and the joint capsule, limiting its excursion and explaining why it tears more frequently than the more mobile lateral meniscus. Vascular supply is zonal: the outer one-third (red-red zone) receives blood from the perimeniscal capillary plexus and can heal; the middle third (red-white zone) has intermediate healing potential; and the inner two-thirds (white-white avascular zone) cannot heal because it depends on synovial fluid diffusion. Orthopedic surgeons and sports medicine physicians manage these injuries, with physiotherapists central to recovery whether or not surgery is performed.

typesClassification.description: A tear running perpendicular to the circumferential collagen fibers, from the inner free edge outward. Disrupts hoop stress transmission and biomechanically behaves like a total meniscectomy if it reaches the periphery. Common in the posterior horn.

A tear that splits the meniscus into superior and inferior leaflets, like opening a book. Typically degenerative, often associated with meniscal cysts on the medial joint line, and seen in patients over 40.

A tear that runs along the long axis of the meniscus parallel to the periphery. When the inner fragment displaces into the intercondylar notch, it produces a true mechanical locked knee. Frequent in acute traumatic tears with associated ACL rupture.
A combination radial and horizontal tear that creates an unstable flap. The flap catches between the femur and tibia, producing intermittent clicking and giving-way.
Multiple tear planes within the same meniscus, typical of long-standing degenerative change. Often coexists with chondral wear and early osteoarthritis. Surgical repair is rarely feasible.

An avulsion of the posterior meniscal root from its tibial attachment. Biomechanically equivalent to a complete meniscectomy because hoop tension is lost. High osteoarthritis progression unless repaired surgically.

primarySymptoms: Sharp or aching pain localized to the medial joint line, reproducible by direct palpation between the femoral condyle and tibial plateau on the inner side of the knee.

Swelling that develops gradually over 6-24 hours after the inciting event, in contrast to the immediate hemarthrosis of an ACL tear; effusion is usually modest unless a chronic mechanical block is present.
Mechanical clicking, popping, or catching with knee flexion-extension, often felt by the patient and audible to an examiner during the McMurray maneuver.
Episodes of true locking, where the knee cannot be fully straightened due to a displaced bucket-handle fragment lodged in the intercondylar notch, requiring manual reduction or surgery.
A feeling of giving-way or instability when twisting, descending stairs, or rising from a deep squat, even though true ligamentous laxity is absent on examination.
Pain provoked by squatting, kneeling, deep flexion, or pivoting on a planted foot; running on level ground is often tolerable while cutting and rotational sport is not.
Posterior medial knee pain that worsens at the end range of flexion, classic for posterior horn tears which are the most common site.
Stiffness and reduced range of motion, particularly loss of terminal extension when a flap or bucket-handle fragment blocks the joint.
Quadriceps wasting and weakness on the affected side after a few weeks of guarded weight-bearing, especially the vastus medialis obliquus.
In degenerative tears, gradual onset of medial knee pain over weeks to months without a recalled injury, often accompanied by stiffness on rising after sitting.
earlyWarningSigns: A distinct pop or tearing sensation during a pivoting or squatting movement, even if pain settles within hours
Persistent medial joint-line tenderness more than 7-10 days after a minor knee twist that was expected to resolve
New clicking, catching, or a transient locking sensation during routine flexion-extension
Inability to fully straighten the knee after a sporting injury or fall
Slow-onset medial knee aching in an adult over 40 without recalled trauma, particularly with stair descent or rising from a deep chair
emergencySigns: Hot, red, swollen knee with fever above 38.5°C — septic arthritis must be excluded urgently with aspiration; meniscal pain alone does not cause systemic illness
Locked knee that cannot be passively reduced — a displaced bucket-handle fragment is causing mechanical block and needs urgent orthopedic review within 1-2 weeks to prevent chondral damage
Acute knee injury with massive tense hemarthrosis within minutes — suggests associated ACL rupture or peripheral meniscal tear in the vascular zone and requires same-week orthopedic assessment
Calf swelling, persistent calf pain, or shortness of breath after a knee injury or recent surgery — deep vein thrombosis or pulmonary embolism must be excluded

causes.cause: Acute rotational injury on a planted foot
causes.description: Sudden twisting of the femur over a fixed tibia, typical in soccer cuts, basketball pivots, skiing falls, and rugby tackles, generates shear forces that exceed the tensile strength of meniscal collagen. Posterior horn injuries dominate because the medial meniscus is least mobile posteriorly.

A valgus blow to a flexed, externally rotated knee tears the anterior cruciate ligament, medial collateral ligament, and medial meniscus together. The classical 'unhappy triad of O'Donoghue' accounts for a substantial fraction of acute sports-related medial tears in young athletes.

Repetitive low-grade loading over decades, combined with age-related dehydration and loss of proteoglycans, weakens the meniscal substance. After age 40 the cumulative microdamage often produces complex or horizontal tears with no specific injury event.

Carpenters, plumbers, miners, and tilers spend hours in extreme flexion. The compressive and shear load on the posterior horn of the medial meniscus rises sharply beyond 120 degrees of flexion and accelerates tear formation.

Each additional unit of body mass index increases meniscal contact stress measurably; the incidence of degenerative medial tears rises roughly 50-60% in patients with a BMI above 30 compared to normal weight controls.

Varus knee alignment (bowleg) shifts load preferentially to the medial compartment, predisposing both to degenerative tears and to early medial osteoarthritis. The meniscus is overloaded for years before symptomatic failure.

riskFactors.description: Englund 2008 NEJM data show meniscal tear prevalence on knee MRI rises from under 10% in adults under 40 to over 50% in men over 70, mostly degenerative and asymptomatic.

riskFactors.factor: Male sex (in young adult traumatic cohorts)
riskFactors.description: Men are 2-3 times more likely to sustain acute meniscal tears in pivoting sports; the sex difference disappears in degenerative tears after age 50.
Soccer, basketball, rugby, skiing, and wrestling account for the majority of acute traumatic meniscal tears. Risk falls substantially with neuromuscular training programs (FIFA 11+, PEP).
Workers with more than 1 hour daily of squatting or kneeling have approximately 2-fold higher rates of medial meniscal tear and downstream osteoarthritis (Coggon 2000 occupational cohort).
Obesity drives both degenerative tears and accelerates post-tear cartilage loss; weight loss of 10% reduces medial compartment contact force by approximately 24%.
An ACL-deficient knee shears the medial meniscus during pivot; up to 80% of chronic ACL-deficient knees develop medial meniscal tears within 5-10 years if left unreconstructed.
Mechanical axis deviation into the medial compartment increases medial meniscal load and is associated with higher rates of medial tear and progression to medial-compartment osteoarthritis.
Loss of meniscal tissue accelerates contact stress; the rate of recurrent or contralateral tear and of osteoarthritis is markedly higher within 10 years of meniscectomy (METEOR 5-year follow-up).
Rare in the medial meniscus (under 1% of the population, more common laterally), but the abnormal shape predisposes to tear at younger ages, including in children.

affectedDemographics: Acute traumatic tears cluster in male athletes aged 18-35; degenerative tears cluster in adults over 40 with no sex predominance
MRI-detectable meniscal abnormalities occur in 19% of women and 56% of men aged 50-90, the majority asymptomatic (Englund 2008 NEJM Framingham data)
Occupational tears concentrate in tradesmen who spend more than an hour daily in deep squat or kneeling postures
Pediatric medial meniscal tears are rare and most often involve a congenital discoid variant or a peripheral tear sustained in a fall
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis combines a focused history, provocative physical examination, and confirmatory imaging. The history establishes mechanism: a single twisting injury with immediate symptoms favors a traumatic tear, whereas insidious medial pain over weeks in a patient over 40 favors a degenerative tear. Examination begins with inspection for effusion and quadriceps wasting, then palpation along the medial joint line, which is tender in roughly 75% of medial tears. Three provocative tests dominate clinical practice: the McMurray test (passive flexion, external rotation, then extension of the tibia, eliciting a painful click — sensitivity 60-80%, specificity 70-80%), the Apley grind test (axial loading with rotation in prone flexion to differentiate meniscal from ligamentous pain), and the Thessaly test (weight-bearing rotation on a 20-degree flexed knee, with reported specificity of about 90% in some series). No single test is definitive; combining two or more raises diagnostic accuracy. Plain weight-bearing X-rays of the knee in extension and the Rosenberg view exclude osteoarthritis, fracture, loose bodies, and joint-space narrowing that would alter management. Magnetic resonance imaging is the diagnostic gold standard: a 1.5T or 3T MRI without contrast detects meniscal tears with approximately 93-95% sensitivity and 88-95% specificity, grades tear morphology, and screens for associated ACL, MCL, chondral, and bone-bruise injuries. Diagnostic arthroscopy is reserved for cases where MRI is equivocal but clinical suspicion remains high, or where intervention is already planned.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Bedside provocative maneuver that compresses and rotates a torn fragment between the femur and tibia, producing a palpable or audible click with pain at the joint line. Sensitivity 60-80%; specificity 70-80%.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The clinician lies the patient supine, flexes the hip and knee, then extends the knee while rotating the tibia internally and externally. The test takes under a minute; mild pain is expected if a tear is present.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Differentiates meniscal from ligamentous pain by axially loading the knee with the patient prone, knee flexed to 90 degrees, while rotating the tibia. Pain on compression suggests meniscal pathology; pain on distraction suggests ligament injury.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The patient lies face-down with the knee bent at a right angle. The clinician applies downward pressure on the heel while rotating the foot. The test is brief and well-tolerated.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Dynamic weight-bearing test for meniscal injury, performed at 5 and 20 degrees of knee flexion. Reported specificity around 90% in initial series, though later studies report lower numbers.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The patient stands on the affected leg with the knee slightly flexed and rotates the body internally and externally three times. A positive test reproduces medial joint-line pain or clicking.

diagnosticTests.test: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the knee
diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold-standard non-invasive imaging. Grades tear morphology (grade 1-3 signal change; grade 3 contacts an articular surface, defining a true tear) and screens for associated ACL, MCL, chondral, and bone-bruise injuries with 93-95% sensitivity and 88-95% specificity.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 30-45 minute scan in a magnet, no radiation, no injection unless an MR arthrogram is indicated. The patient lies still with the knee in a coil; mild noise is normal.

diagnosticTests.test: Weight-bearing knee X-rays (Rosenberg view, lateral, skyline)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes osteoarthritis, fracture, loose bodies, and joint-space narrowing. Essential before considering surgery, because joint-space narrowing of more than 50% predicts poor meniscectomy outcomes.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standing X-rays of both knees in 45-degree flexion (Rosenberg view) plus lateral and skyline views. Total scan time is under 10 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Direct visualization of the meniscus and probing for tear stability. Reserved for equivocal MRI with persistent symptoms or where surgical intervention is already planned in the same session.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A day-case operation under general or regional anesthesia. Two small portal incisions; recovery to walking within 1-3 days. Risks include infection, bleeding, and stiffness.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Supplementary tool to visualize parameniscal cysts associated with horizontal tears and to guide intra-articular injections. Not a primary diagnostic test for the tear itself.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A clinician applies gel and a probe to the joint line; the scan takes 10-15 minutes and is painless. No radiation.

treatmentOverview: Treatment depends on tear morphology, patient age, activity level, and the presence of mechanical symptoms. The AAOS Clinical Practice Guideline on Meniscus Tears and the ISAKOS consensus both endorse a stepwise approach: start with a structured rehabilitation program for stable, non-displaced tears, and reserve surgery for those who fail conservative care or who have a true mechanical block. For degenerative tears in patients over 40 without locking, the evidence is now unambiguous. Three landmark trials — Sihvonen 2013 NEJM (FIDELITY), the METEOR trial (Katz 2013 NEJM), and the ESCAPE trial (van de Graaf 2018 JAMA) — each found that arthroscopic partial meniscectomy is no better than supervised exercise therapy at 6, 12, and 24 months for degenerative meniscal tears. Conservative treatment is therefore first-line in this group. It includes a 6- to 12-week physiotherapy program focused on quadriceps and hip strengthening, especially the vastus medialis obliquus and gluteus medius, plus activity modification and short courses of oral NSAIDs (naproxen 250-500 mg twice daily or ibuprofen 400 mg three times daily) for pain. Intra-articular corticosteroid injection delivers 50-60% short-term symptomatic benefit (4-8 weeks). Platelet-rich plasma and hyaluronic acid injections are still investigational; current evidence does not support routine use. Surgical options include arthroscopic partial meniscectomy (debrides the unstable fragment, preserves the rim, gives 80-90% short-term pain relief but accelerates osteoarthritis at 10 years compared to repair-eligible tissue preserved); arthroscopic meniscal repair (inside-out, outside-in, or all-inside suture techniques, reserved for red-red and good red-white tears in active patients, with 60-90% healing rates depending on tear type, vascularity, and concomitant ACL reconstruction); meniscal allograft transplantation in young post-meniscectomy patients with intact articular cartilage; and root repair for posterior root avulsions. Mechanical locking, displaced bucket-handle tears, and traumatic peripheral tears in the red-red zone are clear surgical indications.

medicalTreatments.name: Structured physiotherapy program (6-12 weeks)
medicalTreatments.description: Supervised quadriceps strengthening (especially vastus medialis obliquus), hip abductor work, proprioceptive retraining, and graded return to activity. First-line for degenerative tears and stable traumatic tears without mechanical symptoms.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: ESCAPE trial: non-inferior to arthroscopic partial meniscectomy at 24 months in patients aged 45-70 with degenerative tears, with roughly 70% of patients returning to baseline function.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral NSAIDs (naproxen 250-500 mg twice daily; ibuprofen 400 mg three times daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Short-course anti-inflammatory therapy for acute pain control during the first 2-4 weeks. Use the lowest effective dose for the shortest duration; check for renal, gastric, and cardiovascular contraindications.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinically meaningful pain reduction in roughly 60-70% of patients during the first 4 weeks.

medicalTreatments.name: Intra-articular corticosteroid injection (methylprednisolone 40 mg or triamcinolone 40 mg with local anesthetic)
medicalTreatments.description: Ultrasound- or landmark-guided injection into the joint for refractory pain after 4-6 weeks of physiotherapy. Limited to two to three injections per year because of chondrotoxic and atrophic risks.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 50-60% report meaningful pain reduction at 4-8 weeks; benefit typically wanes by 12 weeks.

medicalTreatments.name: Activity modification and load management
medicalTreatments.description: Temporary avoidance of deep squatting, kneeling, twisting, and impact running, replaced by cycling, swimming, and elliptical work. Combined with weight reduction in overweight patients to lower medial compartment load.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Used as an adjunct in roughly 70-80% of conservative management programs; difficult to study in isolation but consistently endorsed in clinical guidelines.

medicalTreatments.name: Platelet-rich plasma (PRP) injection
medicalTreatments.description: Investigational biologic therapy delivering concentrated growth factors to the joint. Evidence base is heterogeneous; the AAOS guideline lists it as inconclusive for meniscal tears specifically.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Limited evidence; effect size in symptomatic improvement is modest and similar to corticosteroid injection at 6 months in small trials.

medicalTreatments.name: Hyaluronic acid (viscosupplementation) injection
medicalTreatments.description: Intra-articular hyaluronate injection delivered weekly for 3-5 sessions in some protocols. Mainly studied in osteoarthritis with coexistent meniscal pathology rather than isolated meniscal tear.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Modest pain relief at 3-6 months in mixed osteoarthritis-meniscal populations; effect on isolated meniscal tears is uncertain.

surgicalOptions.name: Arthroscopic partial meniscectomy
surgicalOptions.description: Day-case arthroscopic procedure that resects the torn and unstable portion of the meniscus while preserving as much intact rim as possible. Indicated for mechanical locking, displaced flap or bucket-handle tears, and tears that fail conservative therapy. Returns to walking within days and to most sports by 4-8 weeks.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 80-90% short-term pain relief at 1-2 years; however, METEOR and Sihvonen FIDELITY trials show no advantage over supervised exercise for degenerative tears, and 10-year follow-up shows accelerated medial-compartment osteoarthritis compared to preserved meniscus.

surgicalOptions.name: Arthroscopic meniscal repair (inside-out, outside-in, all-inside)
surgicalOptions.description: Suture or all-inside implant device repair of the tear, restoring meniscal continuity. Reserved for tears in the red-red (outer one-third) and good red-white zones, longitudinal tears under 4 cm, and patients under 50 with no significant chondral wear. Often performed concurrently with ACL reconstruction.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Healing rates of 60-90% depending on tear type, vascularity, and whether ACL reconstruction is performed simultaneously; isolated repairs in avascular zones fare worst. Long-term knee preservation is markedly better than meniscectomy when repair succeeds.

surgicalOptions.name: Meniscal root repair
surgicalOptions.description: Transtibial pull-out suture repair of the avulsed posterior meniscal root. Critical to restore hoop tension and prevent rapid post-injury osteoarthritis. Indicated in active patients with acute root tears and preserved cartilage.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Magnetic resonance and clinical healing in 60-85% at 2 years; conversion to total knee arthroplasty at 10 years falls from over 50% (untreated) to under 10% with successful repair.

surgicalOptions.name: Meniscal allograft transplantation
surgicalOptions.description: Replacement of a previously resected medial meniscus with a sized donor graft, secured with bone plugs or suture. Reserved for symptomatic post-meniscectomy patients under 50 with intact articular cartilage, neutral alignment, and stable ligaments.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Symptomatic improvement in 70-85% at 5 years; graft survival around 70-75% at 10 years. Does not reliably halt long-term osteoarthritis progression but delays it.

surgicalOptions.name: High tibial osteotomy with concurrent meniscal procedure
surgicalOptions.description: Realignment of the tibia to unload the medial compartment, often combined with meniscal repair or transplantation in young patients with varus alignment and isolated medial pathology.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 10-year survivorship around 80-90% in well-selected patients; delays or avoids the need for joint replacement.

alternativeTreatments.name: Neuromuscular training programs (FIFA 11+, PEP)
alternativeTreatments.description: Structured warm-up and neuromuscular conditioning programs reduce non-contact knee injury rates, including meniscal and ACL injuries, in young athletes by roughly 30-50% (Cochrane review, Sadoghi 2012).

alternativeTreatments.name: Targeted weight loss in overweight patients
alternativeTreatments.description: A 10% reduction in body mass index reduces medial compartment contact force by approximately 24% and lowers pain scores meaningfully in adults with degenerative tears and early medial osteoarthritis.

whySeeSpecialist: An orthopedic surgeon or sports medicine physician should be involved when the knee is truly locked or cannot fully extend, when symptoms persist beyond 6-12 weeks of supervised physiotherapy, when MRI shows a displaced bucket-handle, root, or unstable peripheral tear, when there is a coexisting ACL or chondral injury, or when the patient is a young athlete with an acute tear potentially amenable to repair. Primary care or sports physiotherapy is appropriate for first-line assessment and conservative management of stable degenerative tears.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for board certification in orthopedic surgery with subspecialty training in sports medicine or knee surgery, demonstrable experience with meniscal repair (not just meniscectomy), and ideally fellowship training. Ask the surgeon their personal repair-to-resection ratio; a high-volume meniscus surgeon attempting repair whenever tissue allows is preferable to one who routinely defaults to meniscectomy. Confirm access to MRI within the same practice or local network and a structured rehabilitation pathway.
preventionStrategies: Adopt a neuromuscular warm-up program such as FIFA 11+ or PEP before pivoting sports — randomized trials show a 30-50% reduction in non-contact knee injuries including meniscal tears
Maintain a healthy body weight; aim for BMI under 25 to minimize medial compartment overload and reduce degenerative tear risk by approximately 25%
Strengthen the quadriceps and hip abductors continuously, not just after injury — stronger muscle envelopes protect the meniscus during pivot loads
Limit prolonged deep squatting and kneeling at work and home; use knee pads, alternate postures, and microbreaks every 20-30 minutes when unavoidable
Address ACL injuries promptly with reconstruction in active patients, since chronic ACL deficiency dramatically raises later medial meniscal tear risk
lifestyleModifications: Replace impact running with cycling, swimming, or elliptical training during symptomatic periods to maintain fitness without loading the meniscus
Incorporate hip and core strengthening alongside knee-focused work — weak gluteus medius drives dynamic knee valgus during cuts and squats
Choose supportive cushioned footwear, replace running shoes every 500-800 km, and consider lateral wedge insoles if varus alignment is contributing to medial pain
Avoid deep squatting beyond 120 degrees of knee flexion during the recovery phase; partial squats to 60-90 degrees are safer for meniscal load
Use ice for 15-20 minutes after exercise during the symptomatic phase to limit reactive effusion and pain
Maintain a daily home exercise program after formal physiotherapy ends — muscular protection is lost within 4-6 weeks of stopping

recommended: Adequate dietary protein (1.2-1.6 g/kg/day) during rehabilitation to support muscle rebuilding around the knee
Anti-inflammatory Mediterranean-style eating pattern emphasizing oily fish, olive oil, nuts, and vegetables
Omega-3-rich foods (salmon, sardines, flaxseed, walnuts) for general joint anti-inflammatory effect
Calcium and vitamin D adequacy for bone health beneath the meniscus, especially in patients over 50
Adequate hydration to support cartilage and meniscal proteoglycan turnover
avoid: Excess processed sugar and high-glycemic foods that contribute to weight gain and systemic inflammation
Chronic alcohol overuse, which impairs healing capacity and muscle recovery
Crash diets that produce rapid weight loss with disproportionate muscle loss — the quadriceps protects the meniscus
Excessive caloric intake in patients above ideal body weight, given the direct effect of BMI on medial compartment load

exerciseGuidelines: During the acute phase, prioritize relative rest, ice, and gentle range-of-motion drills; avoid loaded deep flexion and pivoting. Begin closed-chain quadriceps strengthening (mini-squats to 60 degrees, leg press to 60 degrees, step-ups) within 1-2 weeks of injury or partial meniscectomy, and within 4-6 weeks of meniscal repair if cleared by the surgeon. Cycling and swimming maintain cardiovascular fitness without shear. Return to pivoting sport requires symmetric quadriceps strength (within 10% of the uninjured leg), full range of motion, no effusion, and a graded running-to-cutting progression — typically 4-8 weeks after partial meniscectomy and 4-6 months after meniscal repair.
dailyManagement: Perform daily home exercises for quadriceps and hip strength even after formal physiotherapy ends — adherence is the strongest predictor of long-term outcome
Monitor for recurrent locking, persistent effusion, or new mechanical symptoms and report them to the surgeon promptly
Use ice for 15-20 minutes after activity during symptomatic phases; heat is more useful for stiffness in chronic disease
Maintain a healthy weight; daily weighing or weekly tracking helps prevent gradual weight gain during reduced activity
Take short walking breaks every 30-45 minutes when sitting for long periods to prevent stiffness and effusion
prognosis: Outcomes vary widely with tear type, treatment choice, and patient factors. Stable degenerative tears managed with structured rehabilitation reach functional baseline in roughly 70% of patients by 6 months, with results equivalent to surgery at 1-2 years (ESCAPE, FIDELITY, METEOR trials). Arthroscopic partial meniscectomy provides 80-90% short-term symptomatic improvement but is associated with progression to symptomatic osteoarthritis in 30-50% of patients at 10-15 years, depending on the amount of meniscus removed and baseline cartilage status. Successful meniscal repair preserves long-term knee function: 10-year reoperation rates are 20-25% for repair compared with 35-45% for meniscectomy in matched cohorts, and rates of symptomatic medial osteoarthritis are substantially lower. Concurrent ACL reconstruction improves meniscal repair healing rates by roughly 10-15 percentage points because of intra-articular blood from drilling. The strongest negative prognostic factors are advanced age, complex tear morphology, avascular zone location, established cartilage damage, varus malalignment, and obesity. The decisive practical message: preserve the meniscus whenever possible.
recoveryTimeline: For arthroscopic partial meniscectomy, full weight-bearing typically resumes within 2-3 days, return to office work within 1 week, return to recreational running by 4-6 weeks, and return to pivoting sport by 6-8 weeks. For meniscal repair, weight-bearing is often restricted for the first 2-4 weeks (depending on tear type and surgeon protocol), range of motion is gradually progressed over 6 weeks, and return to pivoting sport is delayed to 4-6 months to allow tissue healing. Degenerative tears treated conservatively show progressive improvement over 6-12 weeks of supervised physiotherapy, with most functional gains plateauing by month 6.
complications: Progression to medial-compartment osteoarthritis — occurs in 30-50% of patients within 10-15 years of partial meniscectomy, and earlier after complete meniscectomy or untreated root tear
Failed meniscal repair with re-tear — rates of 10-30% depending on tear location, age, and concurrent ACL status; may require revision repair or meniscectomy
Chronic medial knee pain and effusion despite surgery — affects roughly 10-15% of post-meniscectomy patients and often reflects underlying chondral damage
Iatrogenic chondral injury from arthroscopic instruments — uncommon but documented in 1-2% of arthroscopies
Deep vein thrombosis after knee arthroscopy — incidence around 0.3-1% symptomatic; routine prophylaxis is debated and based on patient risk factors
Saphenous nerve neuroma or numbness from medial portal or inside-out repair sutures — occurs in 1-5% of repairs

supportResources.name: American Academy of Orthopaedic Surgeons — Meniscal Tears
supportResources.description: Authoritative US patient-facing education from the body that publishes the meniscus tear clinical practice guideline.

supportResources.name: NHS — Cartilage Damage
supportResources.description: UK National Health Service patient guidance on knee cartilage and meniscal injuries, including when to seek care.

supportResources.name: British Orthopaedic Sports Trauma and Arthroscopy Association (BOSTAA)
supportResources.description: Specialty society resources on knee injury rehabilitation and surgical care.

confusedWithConditions.name: Knee Osteoarthritis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Knee osteoarthritis produces gradually progressive medial pain over years with morning stiffness lasting under 30 minutes, crepitus, and joint-space narrowing on weight-bearing X-rays. A meniscal tear can sit on top of osteoarthritis; mechanical locking and a specific twisting injury favor the meniscal component. Many degenerative meniscal tears found on MRI are part of early osteoarthritis rather than a separate problem.

confusedWithConditions.name: ACL Tear
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: An ACL tear causes immediate massive hemarthrosis within minutes, a feeling of the knee giving way, and a positive Lachman or pivot-shift test. A medial meniscus tear produces slower-onset effusion (6-24 hours) and joint-line tenderness. The two often coexist as part of the unhappy triad; MRI distinguishes them definitively.

confusedWithConditions.name: Lateral Meniscus Tear
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Lateral meniscus tears produce lateral joint-line tenderness, often with discoid meniscus variants in younger patients, and are roughly three times less common than medial tears. The McMurray test elicits pain with internal rather than external tibial rotation.

confusedWithConditions.name: Medial Collateral Ligament Sprain
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: An MCL sprain produces pain and tenderness over the broad medial femoral and tibial origins of the ligament, not the joint line itself, and is provoked by a valgus stress test. MCL pain is usually higher than the joint line and improves quickly with bracing; meniscal pain persists at the joint line and is reproduced by squatting and rotation.

relatedConditions.name: ACL Tear
relatedConditions.relevance: ACL injury and medial meniscus tear classically occur together (the unhappy triad); chronic ACL deficiency markedly raises later medial meniscal tear risk.

relatedConditions.name: Osteoarthritis
relatedConditions.relevance: Degenerative medial meniscal tears are often early manifestations of medial-compartment osteoarthritis, and meniscectomy accelerates osteoarthritis progression.

relatedConditions.name: Lateral Meniscus Tear
relatedConditions.relevance: The lateral meniscus is the other half of the meniscal cushion; tear patterns, repair techniques, and rehabilitation overlap closely with medial tears.

relatedConditions.name: Hip Labral Tear
relatedConditions.relevance: Hip and knee labrocartilaginous injuries share mechanisms (pivot under load) and overlapping presentations in athletes.

relatedConditions.name: Rotator Cuff Tear
relatedConditions.relevance: Another common partial tear of fibrocartilaginous tissue managed by orthopedic surgeons; principles of tissue preservation and graded rehabilitation are analogous.

faqs.question: Do I need surgery for a medial meniscus tear?
faqs.answer: Not always. Stable degenerative tears usually do well with 6-12 weeks of physiotherapy; FIDELITY, METEOR, and ESCAPE trials showed no advantage of surgery over exercise at 1-2 years. Surgery is reserved for a locked knee, bucket-handle tears, root tears, and acute athlete injuries.

faqs.question: What does a torn medial meniscus feel like?
faqs.answer: Sharp or aching pain on the inner side of the knee that worsens with squatting, twisting, or stair descent. There is often a popping or clicking sensation, gradual swelling within 6-24 hours, and sometimes a feeling that the knee briefly locks or gives way.

faqs.question: Can a medial meniscus tear heal on its own?
faqs.answer: Tears in the outer one-third (red-red vascular zone) can heal, especially small longitudinal tears under 1 cm. Tears in the inner two-thirds (avascular white-white zone) cannot heal. Most degenerative tears do not heal but often become asymptomatic with rehabilitation.

faqs.question: How long does recovery from meniscus surgery take?
faqs.answer: Arthroscopic partial meniscectomy: walking in 2-3 days, office work in 1 week, pivoting sport by 6-8 weeks. Meniscal repair takes longer because tissue must heal: weight-bearing is restricted 2-4 weeks and return to pivoting sport delayed to 4-6 months.

faqs.question: What is the difference between meniscus repair and meniscectomy?
faqs.answer: Meniscectomy removes the torn portion; recovery is fast but the procedure accelerates osteoarthritis over 10-15 years. Meniscal repair stitches the tear together, takes longer to rehabilitate, and is only feasible for tears in the vascular zone — but preserves the joint long term.

faqs.question: What is the unhappy triad?
faqs.answer: The unhappy triad of O'Donoghue describes simultaneous injury to the ACL, the medial collateral ligament, and the medial meniscus, usually from a lateral blow to a flexed externally rotated knee. It typically requires ACL reconstruction with concurrent meniscal repair.

faqs.question: Why does the medial meniscus tear more often than the lateral?
faqs.answer: The medial meniscus is anchored to the medial collateral ligament and joint capsule, which limits its movement during knee rotation. The lateral meniscus is more mobile and can shift out of the way. The medial meniscus therefore tears roughly three times as often.

faqs.question: Can I run with a medial meniscus tear?
faqs.answer: Running on level ground is often tolerable with a stable tear once acute swelling settles. Pivoting, cutting sports, and deep squatting are usually problematic. If running reproduces pain or causes swelling, switch to cycling or swimming and complete a rehab program first.

faqs.question: What is a bucket-handle meniscus tear?
faqs.answer: A longitudinal tear in which the inner fragment flips into the intercondylar notch like a bucket handle. It typically causes true mechanical locking, with the knee unable to fully straighten. Surgery to reduce and ideally repair the fragment is usually needed within a few weeks.

faqs.question: Does an MRI always show a meniscus tear?
faqs.answer: Modern MRI detects meniscal tears with about 93-95% sensitivity and 88-95% specificity, but some small tears are missed and some signal changes are not true tears. If symptoms persist despite a normal MRI, repeat imaging or diagnostic arthroscopy may be considered.

faqs.question: Will I get arthritis after a meniscus tear?
faqs.answer: Risk depends on tear type and treatment. Partial meniscectomy carries roughly 30-50% risk of symptomatic medial osteoarthritis at 10-15 years. Successful meniscal repair preserves the cushioning function and lowers — but does not eliminate — long-term osteoarthritis risk.

faqs.question: How much does meniscus surgery cost?
faqs.answer: In the US, arthroscopic partial meniscectomy typically costs 5,000-10,000 US dollars and is usually covered by insurance for indicated cases. In India, the procedure commonly ranges 80,000-200,000 rupees in private hospitals; public hospital care is far cheaper.

faqs.question: Can a meniscus tear cause knee locking?
faqs.answer: Yes. A displaced bucket-handle or flap tear can lodge a meniscal fragment between the femur and tibia, blocking knee extension. Locking is a clear surgical indication and should prompt urgent orthopedic assessment to prevent cartilage damage.

faqs.question: Should I have surgery if my tear is degenerative?
faqs.answer: Current evidence favors a trial of physiotherapy first. The FIDELITY and ESCAPE trials showed that for degenerative tears without locking, exercise gives equivalent outcomes to surgery at 1-2 years. Surgery remains appropriate if symptoms fail to improve after 3-6 months of rehab.

faqs.question: Can children get medial meniscus tears?
faqs.answer: Yes, but they are uncommon and often involve a congenital discoid variant or a peripheral tear from a fall. Healing potential is much better in children because of richer meniscal vascularity, so repair is strongly preferred over resection to preserve the meniscus for life.

faqs.question: What injections help with a meniscus tear?
faqs.answer: Corticosteroid injection gives short-term pain relief in 50-60% of patients for 4-8 weeks. Hyaluronic acid has modest benefit at 3-6 months mainly in mixed osteoarthritis cases. Platelet-rich plasma is investigational. None of these injections heal the tear; they treat pain.

faqs.question: How is a meniscus tear graded on MRI?
faqs.answer: MRI signal change is graded 1 to 3. Grades 1 and 2 indicate internal degeneration that does not reach the surface and is not a true tear. Grade 3 signal contacts an articular surface and represents a true tear. Morphology and zone are also described to guide treatment.

faqs.question: Is meniscus repair always possible?
faqs.answer: No. Repair is feasible mainly for tears in the vascular outer one-third (red-red zone), longitudinal tears under 4 cm, and patients under 50 with healthy cartilage. Avascular-zone, complex degenerative, and older tears are rarely repairable and are usually debrided.

faqs.question: Can a meniscus tear cause swelling weeks after the injury?
faqs.answer: Yes. Mechanical irritation from an unstable flap or root tear can produce recurrent effusions days to weeks after the injury, especially after exercise. Persistent effusion beyond a few weeks despite rest and ice warrants imaging and orthopedic review.

faqs.question: When can I drive after meniscus surgery?
faqs.answer: Most patients can drive 1-2 weeks after right-knee meniscectomy, once they can perform an emergency stop and are off opioid analgesia. Right-knee meniscal repair may delay safe driving to 4-6 weeks. Left-knee surgery on an automatic vehicle usually permits earlier return.

images.altText: Anatomical diagram of the right knee joint showing the medial and lateral menisci between the femur and tibia, with cruciate and collateral ligaments labeled
images.caption: Knee joint anatomy showing the medial meniscus tethered to the medial collateral ligament and joint capsule.

images.altText: Sagittal MRI of the knee demonstrating a high-signal tear through the posterior horn of the medial meniscus reaching the articular surface
images.caption: Sagittal MRI showing a grade-3 tear of the posterior horn of the medial meniscus.

images.altText: Clinical examination of a knee with medial joint-line tenderness, the classic finding in a medial meniscus tear
images.caption: Medial joint-line tenderness on examination — the most reproducible sign of a medial meniscus tear.
